data.title: Trump-Powell Battle Escalates: Will It Affect Markets?
data.description:
Late on Sunday, January 11, 2026, Chairman Jerome Powell released an explicitly worded and formal statement informing the world that he is under criminal investigation by the U.S. Department of Justice.

Powell was bold, assertive, and direct when he said the following:

This new threat is not about my testimony last June or about the renovation of the Federal Reserve buildings. It is not about Congress's oversight role; the Fed through testimony and other public disclosures made every effort to keep Congress informed about the renovation project. Those are pretexts. The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.

In my view, if Powell is indicted, or if the investigation pressures him enough to cut rates, even when economic conditions are stable, markets may respond negatively. He is most likely aware of this, which may have prompted him to make the following statements:

This is about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions—or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.

I have served at the Federal Reserve under four administrations, Republicans and Democrats alike. In every case, I have carried out my duties without political fear or favor, focused solely on our mandate of price stability and maximum employment. Public service sometimes requires standing firm in the face of threats. I will continue to do the job the Senate confirmed me to do, with integrity and a commitment to serving the American people.

Translation: Powell has clearly indicated that this threat (his words, not mine) is not substantial enough to deter him, and consequently the Fed, away from a data-driven approach.

Such an immediate and clear statement should ensure that market participants aren’t blindsided when global economies recommence activities on Monday.

The Fed Isn’t a Dictatorship

This escalation sets a concerning precedent for not only the future Fed chair but also present and future governors and regional Fed presidents - or anyone else involved in FOMC decisions, for that matter.

I believe that no individual in their right mind would prefer to be under investigation, especially a criminal investigation. So, can the possibility of such pressure in the future prove effective?

I don't think so because the Fed is not a dictatorship wherein the chairman can unilaterally decide future monetary policy.

But, in the 1970s, coordination did happen between U.S. President Nixon and Federal Reserve Chairman Burns, a case presented in this research paper titled How Richard Nixon Pressured Arthur Burns, published by the American Economic Association.

Arthur F. Burns was a close ally of Nixon who instructed him to engage in an expansionary monetary policy before the 1972 elections. Burns complied, even though there wasn’t any need to do so, by developing a framework advocating that monetary policy is not the driving force behind inflation.

Rate cuts were thus aimed at improving the labor market and quietly improving the odds of Nixon’s re-election. (Please read this paper for more details.)

But the Fed back in the 70s wasn’t as publicly transparent as it is now. Specifically, the Federal Reserve Reform Act of 1977 made the Fed more accountable and its actions more transparent after that Nixon-Burns episode led to stagflation in the 1970s.

That Reform Act directed the Fed to maintain stable growth, inflation, and employment and mandated that it present Congress with semi-annual reports on its monetary policy plans to achieve those objectives—a requirement still in place.

Lastly, the Fed now publicly announces its decisions, which was not the case until February 1995. These public policy announcements, and the Q&A sessions with established journalists that follow, serve as a strong deterrent for decisions that aren’t consistent with prevailing economic conditions.

If, however, a Fed Chair were to employ a policy inconsistent with economic evidence, market participants would pick up on that immediately and eventually price it in. In such a case, it is highly likely that markets react negatively and may not recover until the Fed’s credibility returns.

No More Rate Cuts

Lastly, I think that this would be an opportune time to discuss whether Powell would cut rates in the next FOMC meeting - irrespective of this latest investigation.

In my latest analysis on Bitcoin, I argued that rate cuts since September 2025 have been precautionary rather than stimulatory. And given that precautionary rate cuts, historically, have totaled 75 bps and that the latest Summary of Economic Projections doesn't indicate an imminent recession and uncontrolled inflation, further rate cuts are improbable

Additionally, "precautionary" indicates that Powell is cutting not because he wants to stimulate the economy or even the labor market, but to ensure that conditions don't deteriorate further.

As a result, even if unemployment and payrolls were to remain stable, which they have, as indicated by BLS’ latest data, with payrolls increasing by 50,000 and unemployment steady at 4.4 percent in December, Powell will halt the current rate cuts cycle.

In Closing

Nothing has changed: rate cuts are not required, and the Trump administration’s highly unusual actions against Powell, in my opinion, are inconsequential unless he has actually committed a criminal act.

Things are likely to go on as usual, and readers shouldn’t alter their portfolios based on this news alone.

data.title: Liberty Energy: The Pivot To A Power Company Is Cash Negative In The Near Term
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Liberty Energy Inc. (LBRT) is a mostly U.S.-focused oilfield services (or OFS) company that has been the second largest supplier of completions services (i.e., fracking), behind Halliburton Company (HAL). At least until recently.

Over the past quarters, management has aggressively pivoted from being an "oil company" to becoming a "power company." The idea is to bring Liberty's expertise in off-grid power solutions, acquired in powering electric frac and other oilfield operations, to a broader industrial customer base that includes data centers, among other use cases.

The company has branded the new offering as LPI, or Liberty Power Innovations:

Providing natural gas-fueled power generation and fueling solutions, LPI delivers dispatchable, distributed energy. Our generators are designed for modular deployment, supporting baseload, backup, and peak power needs, with fuel efficiency and low emissions that outperform conventional diesel alternatives. LPI’s ability to seamlessly integrate microgrid-ready solutions allows us to deliver scalable, adaptable power wherever it’s needed.

Source: Liberty Energy.

Connecting Liberty's story to the dominant AI/data center theme has been very positive for the stock price, which has outperformed the OFS sector (OIH):

The re-rating is not with rationale as the power business is less cyclical than frac demand and should merit a higher multiple.

The downside, however, is that LPI requires significant capex, which will push Liberty into negative free cash flow (or FCF) territory, resulting in higher debt. The investment may pay off, but it comes at the opportunity cost of giving up buybacks or growing the dividend. Further, any slowdown in the AI capex cycle could boomerang on Liberty's now higher multiples.

Considering both sides, I see Liberty as a "hold" at this time.

Don't write off the core frac business

The incentive to be considered an AI-enabling company is strong, especially given that oil has been in a bear market for three years now. U.S. frac fleets in particular keep sliding:

There are a couple of important trends under the surface, though. First, the headline frac spread count reported by Primary Vision is becoming less meaningful as an oilfield activity indicator. As completions designs evolve and operators now frac two, three, or even more wells at the same time while well lateral lengths and proppant loading increase too, the pumping horsepower packaged into the average fleet has grown:

The frac demand measured in horsepower has been more resilient than the frac fleet count.

Second, the frac supply has been bifurcated into older, diesel-run equipment and newer, dual-fuel and electric fleets that can run on natural gas and are preferred by operators from an efficiency perspective. Upgrading frac units from diesel to electric is not cheap, but for Liberty and a couple of other premium operators, this capex upgrade cycle is already in the past. This allows Liberty to maintain higher utilization for its fleets while smaller operators, unable to pay for these upgrades, are forced to idle or scrap their equipment.

When the U.S. shale oil cycle turns around, Liberty Energy will be incredibly well-positioned.

Analysts appear overly excited about LPI

Liberty's announcement last week on a potentially 1GW power deal with data-center operator Vantage was received very well by the market. Sell-side analysts have quoted $300k per megawatt annual EBITDA contribution, so a 1GW deal could theoretically add $300 million in EBITDA—quite material, given even at the top in 2023, Liberty's EBITDA was about $1.2 billion.

The Vantage buildout will be realized over at least five years, so this EBITDA won't come any time soon, but the data center news is so "hot" that analysts don't seem as interested in the core frac business that will continue paying the bills. In the Q3 earnings call, of about 15 questions asked in total, all related to LPI in some aspect, and zero had to do with frac fleets.

UBS, as reported by Seeking Alpha, believes LPI will contribute 28% of Liberty's EBITDA by 2028 and values the power business at $12 per share that is not yet "priced in."

Citi, also quoted by Seeking Alpha, puts the LPI contribution to 35-40% of EBITDA after the power business fully ramps up:

[Onsite] generation appears primed to ramp given accelerating AI investment, but at a pace that utility-scale additions would struggle to match. Onsite generation is the next technology that could scale, and we believe LBRT will participate.

Source: Seeking Alpha.

The sell-side price targets do not appear particularly aggressive, but the stock has also rapidly appreciated in recent months:

All this is not to say I do not believe LPI will succeed, but it is probably good to be a bit cautious when all analysts turn from bearish to bullish on news that may not materialize for a few years out.

Negative cash flow is the price of rebranding

What is quite certain, though, is that LPI will require significant capex investments for Liberty. In fact, consensus estimates now project negative cash flow until 2028, with the capex essentially being funded by debt increases:

While many energy companies have been gradually deleveraging, Liberty plans to rely on debt to fund its expansion into the power sector. In all fairness, much of that will be non-recourse project-level debt backed by power purchase agreements (or PPAs) with Liberty's data center customers:

Those project companies will be funded via debt that is backed by the PPA, ESA. Think about the fact that most likely, that will be project-specific debt, maybe around -- you could get to approximately 70% of the capital needs by debt. It will be nonrecourse to the corporation.

Source: Management comments on Q3 earnings call.

Still, these PPAs are only as good as the solvency of their counterparty. Even tech giants such as Oracle Corporation (ORCL) have seen debt pressures recently, so this will be an area to pay attention to.

By tying itself to the AI theme, Liberty will also benefit relatively less from an oil and gas rebound. The opportunity cost of the LPI capex lies in buybacks that didn't happen or dividends that didn't get paid out. In contrast, Patterson-UTI Energy, Inc. (PTEN), one of Liberty's main competitors in the frac space that is so far staying on the sidelines of the AI power business, is expected to maintain solid double-digit FCF yields over the next years.

Risks to consider

The core frac business remains exposed to oil and gas prices and oilfield capex.

The primary risk with the LPI business, in my view, is that we are too early in the AI journey to know for sure if the "hockey stick" power demand projections will materialize.

A recent Yahoo Finance article featured the AI race from China's perspective. While China has an advantage in terms of lower power costs and better infrastructure, the constraint for Chinese researchers has been the limited access to advanced chips and chip-making machines. This has, in turn, pushed them to develop more efficient algorithms that don't require as many chips.

My concern from LPI's perspective would be if power supply indeed becomes the limiting factor for AI developers in the U.S., it may also force American research teams to develop more efficient algorithms that require less power consumption. It is simply too early to tell how all this will play out.

Takeaway

I remain bullish on Liberty's core frac business given the company's dominant position as a supplier of premium equipment in the U.S. market and fading competition.

However, the trifecta of overwhelming Wall Street sell-side enthusiasm over the new LPI business, negative FCF that will be funded by debt, and the AI theme likely being overdue for some correction gives me pause before recommending Liberty as a "buy".

data.title: Carter's Stock Looks On Sale, But It Really Isn't
data.description:
Nearly two years ago, in February of 2024, I decided to downgrade shares of Carter's, Inc. (CRI) from a buy to a hold. This came after a window of time during which the stock underperformed the market. Financial performance was worsening, with revenue and profits declining. And even though shares of the company were attractively priced, a more bullish assessment did not adequately reflect the overall risks that I perceived. Fast forward to today, and things have gotten a lot worse. Revenue continues to drop. But in addition to this, the company is having to contend with a massive impact caused by tariffs.

The good news is that the stock is still cheap. But the bad news is that part of this is because the share price dropped 52% while the S&P 500 is up 39.1%. This is disappointing to say the least. Now, normally, as a value investor, I would be attracted to something like this. However, that's only the case when I can see the light at the end of the tunnel. And right now, with the data that's currently available, I am not convinced enough to take that leap. So, at best, all I can do is maintain it as a hold candidate.

Things have worsened

Given that we are in January of 2026, I don't think it would be terribly beneficial to focus too much on what happened before 2025. But so that you have the appropriate context, in the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. The bottom line here is that revenue, profits, and cash flow metrics all declined for the business. I would have hoped that 2025 would have been better for the company. But if anything, it was more of the same, but on steroids.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year. Revenue of $1.97 billion was just a hair below the $1.98 billion the company reported the previous year. That's not an awful decline. And in fact, some parts of the company did quite well on the top line. Specifically, the U.S. Retail segment of the company reported a growth in revenue from $950.9 million to $956.3 million. According to management, this increase was driven by new retail stores that the company opened, a nice increase in the units per transaction both in its stores and online, and growth in its core baby assortment category. All of this is great to see.

This was not the only area of expansion that the company exhibited. The International segment reported a rise in revenue from $277.5 million to $289.8 million. This increase, according to the company, can be attributed to improved demand with its international wholesale partners, as well as growth that the company exhibited in Mexico and Canada. Mexico's expansion was driven by an increase in retail stores and a growth in traffic. And in Canada, the company improved because of higher traffic and a rise in units sold.

The problem for the business ended up being its U.S. Wholesale segment. This part of the company was hit, with revenue falling from $756 million to $726.9 million. Management blamed this on changes in the company's business model centered around its Simply Joys brand that, unfortunately, reduced visibility, traffic, and demand for the brand. Lower replenishment and demand with department stores, and a decline in the average sales price of the goods for the company, also proved problematic.

If all we were dealing with was a small revenue decline, I would be inclined to say that perhaps the company was finally stabilizing. But the big problem is on the bottom line. The firm went from a net profit of $124 million to a net profit of only $27.6 million. Other profitability metrics also took a hit. Operating cash flow was negative to the tune of $136.3 million. The year before, it was positive by $11.3 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, it was cut by almost half from $201.1 million to $106.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company dropped from $214.2 million to $127.4 million.

Some of these changes on the bottom line can be chalked up to foreign currency fluctuations, decreased prices for some items, and the like. It seems to me as though tariffs are the big pain point for the company. During the third quarter on its own, the company incurred around $20 million of tariff costs. Management mentioned in their earnings call for the third quarter that annualized incremental gross impacts from tariffs should be somewhere between $200 million and $250 million. For a company that generated net profits of $185.5 million in 2024, that is astronomical. Management is working to counteract this. For instance, the company is working on a $45 million cost reduction plan and has decided, as part of that, to increase the number of stores it is closing from 100 locations to 150.

Management is making other changes as well. It is working, for instance, to achieve cost-sharing agreements with some of its vendors. It is also shifting its production out of India and into other countries like Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Ethiopia. These are nations with lower tariffs. Price increases have also been implemented. And the company is even making changes to its product assortment and packaging. We don't really have much in the way of guidance for 2026. Management did say that we expect net revenue and earnings to improve year over year. But we really don't have specific details on the extent to which this would be the case.

With that in mind, if we project our results based on what we have seen already for the 2025 fiscal year, we would get net profits of about $41.3 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $152.4 million, and EBITDA of $203.3 million. Armed with these estimates and also with the historical results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, except for the price-to-earnings multiple, these are very attractive multiples. This would normally elicit a very solid ‘buy’ rating from me. This is especially true considering that while the stock is expensive on a price-to-earnings basis compared to other similar firms, it is actually quite cheap compared to the competition when it comes to the other valuation approaches. In the chart below, I compared it to five such enterprises. Four of them are cheaper on a price-to-earnings basis. But only two were cheaper when it came to the price-to-operating cash flow basis, while one was cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis.

Takeaway

As tantalizing as Carter's looks from a valuation standpoint, it's not a company that I can get behind right now. The stock is, quite frankly, rather risky. The tariff situation is disconcerting. Even though management has made promises regarding 2026, I need to see more details before getting excited about that. The firm has a history of declining revenue and profits before this point as well. And I do worry about a permanent decline on that front. In fact, it's only because of how cheap the stock is that I believe that assigning it a speculative ‘hold’ rating as opposed to something more bearish makes sense.

data.title: Metals Enter A New Era
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

More diverse demand drivers

China remains a key consumer, but it no longer dominates global metals demand as it once did. Today, demand is more sector-specific and shaped by electrification, clean energy, defence spending and data infrastructure rather than broad-based industrial expansion. However, this shift is uneven, policy-driven and highly sensitive to technology choices and regulation.

The shift is reshaping demand across the metals complex. Metals closely linked to electrification are increasingly influenced by policy incentives and technology adoption rather than traditional industrial cycles.

The metals leading the rally right now are the ones critical for global electrification.

Silver rallied past $80/oz for the first time in history as 2025 drew to a close, following a rally of over 25% in December alone. Copper also hit a series of all-time highs in an end-of-the-year rally, climbing 42% in 2025, making it the best performer of the six industrial metals on the LME.

Copper remains central to grid expansion, renewables and EV infrastructure, while silver’s role in solar panels and advanced electronics has raised its strategic importance. Both metals have been included in the US Geological Survey List of Critical Minerals.

New sources of demand are also emerging, with data centres and AI infrastructure becoming major consumers of metals such as copper and aluminium.

Rising defence spending across multiple regions is boosting the need for aluminium, titanium, copper and specialty alloys.

By contrast, more 'traditional' metals that are still heavily linked to construction, infrastructure, and global manufacturing cycles, like iron ore or zinc, remain more sensitive to cyclical economic conditions.

Supply is less elastic

Years of underinvestment, declining ore grades and longer permitting timelines mean that higher prices no longer deliver rapid supply responses. Environmental and social constraints are reinforcing this rigidity, particularly in developed economies.

Resource nationalism has become a defining feature of metals markets. Indonesia’s ban on nickel ore exports and China’s tighter rare earth export controls are all part of a broader trend. Governments are actively reshaping supply chains to capture more value and secure strategic materials, making supply both slower and riskier. Even when prices rise, new tonnes arrive slowly and often from jurisdictions facing higher geopolitical or ESG risk.

The result is a market where tightness persists longer, price spikes are more likely, and volatility is structurally higher.

Geopolitics takes centre stage

Critical minerals are increasingly viewed as strategic assets. Measures like export controls and stockpiling have become central to industrial policy.

The last few years have seen multiple instances of policy intervention affecting metals flows, especially for critical raw materials, including China’s export restrictions on rare earths, gallium, germanium, and antimony, as well as broader trade and technology controls.

Within this context, governments are accelerating efforts to build reserves of critical minerals. In the US, the Pentagon is seeking to procure up to $1bn worth of critical minerals as part of a global stockpiling spree, including cobalt, antimony, tantalum and scandium, to counter supply chain risks, especially from over-reliance on single suppliers like China.

Similarly, the European Union is actively moving to stockpile critical raw materials through its new RESourceEU Action Plan, with joint purchasing and stockpiling initiatives starting in 2026. This initiative is building on the Critical Raw Materials Act (CRMA), aiming to secure materials vital for green tech and defence.

Unlike commercial demand, stockpiling is price-insensitive and occurs in distinct procurement cycles, adding a new layer of volatility.

As more governments accelerate efforts to bolster national stockpiles, it is likely to become a persistent feature of metal markets' dynamics.

Markets that once reacted primarily to economic data are now increasingly shaped by diplomacy, sanctions and industrial policy - a structural shift reshaping pricing dynamics and global flows.

The new metals playbook

Taken together, these forces suggest the next metals cycle will look fundamentally different from those of the past.

The new phase for metals markets will be shaped by diversified demand sources driven by electrification, defence and digital infrastructure, slower supply responses amid more constrained and politicised supply, increased volatility from geopolitics and decarbonisation, and the growing role of stockpiling and defence spending.

Understanding these drivers will be central to navigating metals markets in 2026 and beyond.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: New Push Against The Fed Sends Dollar, U.S. Bonds And Stocks Lower, Helps Lift Gold And Silver To Record Highs
data.description:
The US dollar (DXY) initially opened firmer, while Japan is on holiday. But news that the Justice Department served grand jury subpoenas to the Federal Reserve on Friday over the renovations of the Fed’s headquarters roiled the markets. Fed Chair Powell issued a terse statement rejecting the charges and said they were a “pretext” for dissatisfaction with the Fed’s interest rate policy. The dollar, US Treasuries and equities have been sold, and the yield curve steepened.

These developments cut short what we had expected to be a further dollar recovery from its late November through late December slide. We anticipated it to be encouraged by the acceptable jobs report and this week’s anticipated firm CPI and improved retail sales and industrial production. Meanwhile, on the geopolitical front, the continued protests in Iran threaten to topple the regime, and this seems to have eclipsed Venezuelan developments.

Prices

G10

• The Dollar Index is snapping a four-day advance today. Since Christmas eve day, it is the second losing session. It reached 99.26 before the weekend, its highest level since December 9. It has been sold to 98.70 today to ease back below the 200-day moving average (98.80).

• The euro’s 0.40% rally through the European morning is the largest gain since December 22. Some buying may have been option-related as options for nearly 1.1 bln euros at $1.1650 expire today. The euro reached nearly $1.1700 in early European turnover. Intraday support is in the $1.1670-75 area. The pre-weekend high was slightly above $1.1660.

• The dollar rose to a new high against the yen near JPY158.20 early today before it was broadly sold. It fell to almost JPY157.50. It tried to move higher against and set a lower high. The greenback has not been above JPY158 in European turnover. Options for $1.45 bln expire there in North America today.

• Sterling is posting a potential bullish outside up day. It traded on both sides of last Friday’s range and is above its high (~$1.3450). It reached the session high near $1.3475 in the European morning. Resistance is seen in the $1.3480-1.3500 area.

• The greenback has been confined to the pre-weekend range against the Canadian dollar (~CAD1.3860-1.3920). Support may be in the CAD1.3840 area, where almost $560 mln in options expire.

• The Australian dollar has recovered from last Friday’s low near $0.6665 to nearly $0.6715. The intraday momentum indicators are stretched, but the upside potential may be extended to the $0.6730 area in North America.

EM

• The dollar was turned back ahead of the weekend near MXN18.04. It settled below MXN18.00 and was sold to MXN17.8965 today. A shelf was forged in the first several days of the year near MXN17.87.

• The PBOC set the dollar’s fix today at CNY7.0108, a new low since October 2024. The greenback was sold to CNH7.9660 against the offshore yuan, a new low since May 2023. We suspect it can fall toward CNH6.80.

• Reports indicate that the failure of Indian PM Modi to call President Trump has scuttled an early trade deal to ease the 50% tariffs levied on the world’s most populous country. The Indian rupee is little changed in quiet turnover.

• Indications suggest the dollar initially rose by about 4.1% against the Venezuelan bolivar last week. It was the 23rd consecutive weekly gain.

• The dollar slipped almost by almost 0.70% against the Argentine peso last week, its first weekly decline in five weeks. Treasury Secretary Bessent claimed victory, indicating that Argentina paid back what it had borrowed under the swap line and that American taxpayers made tens of millions of dollars. The Exchange Stabilization Fund no longer has peso exposure.

Other Markets

• Equities: The large Asia-Pacific bourses rallied today, led by a 1.4% rally in Hong Kong and a 1.9% gain in the index of mainland shares that trade there. After advancing nearly 1% before the weekend, Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is nursing a small loss. US index futures are off 0.6-0.80%.

• European benchmark 10-year yields are narrowly mixed but mostly softer, while the 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up nearly three basis points to approach 4.20%.

• Gold reached a new record high near $4600. Silver has also reached a new record (~$84.60).

• February WTI reversed lower after ticking above the pre-weekend high to $59.80. It is near $58.50 in late European morning turnover.

Data

• With the US labor market apparently not deteriorating quickly and still stuck in what Fed Chair Powell characterized as a low-hiring, low-firing phase, the focus turns to the elevated consumer prices. December CPI will be reported tomorrow. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a 0.3% rise in both the headline and core measures, which would put the year-over-year increase at 2.7%. The market accepts that the Fed is on hold, and the futures market did not have a cut fully discounted until June. Richmond and Atlanta Fed Presidents speak today, but they spoke before the weekend as well. New York Fed President Williams also speaks today. Despite the unusual dispersion of views reflected in the Summary of Economic Projections, there does seem to be a wide consensus of the near-term Fed outlook. Note, the Supreme Court hears the arguments about presidential authority to fire a Federal Reserve governor (Cook) next week.

• First thing tomorrow, Japan reports November’s current account surplus. Although the surplus widened in two of the past three Novembers, over the past 20 years, it has deteriorated in 16 years. Still, the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey looks for a modest widening that can be fully accounted for by the improved trade balance. In the first 10 months of 2025, on a balance-of-payments basis, Japan recorded an average monthly trade deficit of almost JPY153 bln. This is a bit less than half of the January-October 2024 average shortfall of JPY383 bln.

• The 1.0% rise in Australia’s November household spending followed the 1.4% (1.3% initially) rise in October and underscores why the central bank’s short monetary easing cycle is over. It is the strongest two-month increase in a few years. The six-month average through October was 0.6%, matching the best performance since early 2023. And this is within the context of elevated consumer inflation expectations (according to the Melbourne Institute, 4.7% in December vs. 4.5% in November) and firm CPI (November 3.4% headline and 3.2% trimmed mean).

• India reported that the year-over-year rate of CPI nearly doubled to 1.33% from almost 0.7% in November. Food deflation eased as the base effects faded. Rising precious metal prices underpinned core inflation. Still, the impact of September’s cut in goods and services taxes continues to be felt. A new inflation series begins this month, and notably, precious metals are replaced by jewelry.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Air Products and Chemicals: A Turnaround Worth Buying
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

As an analyst, Dividend Aristocrats are some of my favorite stocks to cover. Why is that the case?

Well, it takes a consistent business model for an S&P 500 index (SP500) company to earn and maintain Dividend Aristocrat status. This is because a company must raise its payout for at least 25 consecutive years to fulfill the criteria.

If underlying earnings growth/predictability isn't there, a company simply can't ascend to Dividend Aristocrat status. In that sense, this is an automatic litmus test for business quality.

A promising future earnings growth outlook, investment-grade balance sheet, and manageable payout are the factors that I like to assess to discern whether a company can keep its Dividend Aristocrat status.

This brings me to today's focus, Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD). When Kody last covered APD with a "Buy" rating in October, he liked that the timeline for NEOM was on track. He also thought its focus on becoming a more efficient enterprise was a plus. The A-rated balance sheet was another positive. Lastly, shares were trading at a double-digit percentage discount to his fair value estimate.

Two and a half months later, I'm reaffirming Kody's "Buy" rating. APD unveiled an even bigger global cost reduction plan with the release of its Q4 2025 financial results in November. The company's net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio remains respectable. Even after recalibrating Kody's fair value estimate, shares are still moderately undervalued.

Long-Term Growth Prospects Are Intact

On Nov. 6, APD shared its earnings report for the fiscal fourth quarter ended Sept. 30, 2025. The company's sales decreased by 0.6% year-over-year to $3.17 billion during the quarter. This was $10 million less than Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter.

What contributed to these results for the fiscal fourth quarter? The biggest factor behind APD's slight decline in sales had to do with a 5% drop in volume during the quarter. This was due to a trifecta of headwinds: The sale of its LNG business in the prior year, an Americas prior year early contract termination, and lower helium volume.

Helium prices continued to face pressure during the fiscal fourth quarter. However, that was offset by an improvement in non-helium products pricing. This explains the overall flat price mix for the business in the quarter.

Higher energy cost pass-through lifted sales by 3% for the fiscal fourth quarter. Favorable currency translation also helped sales to the tune of 1% during the quarter.

APD's adjusted EPS decreased by 4.8% over the year-ago period to $3.39 in the fiscal fourth quarter. That edged out Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus by $0.01 for the quarter.

For FY 2025, the company's adjusted EPS declined by 3.2% year-over-year to $12.03. That was $0.03 above its midpoint guidance of $12 ($11.85 to $12.15). Adjusting for the LNG divestiture and the 2% headwind from project exits, adjusted EPS would have grown by 3% in FY 2025 (and even more without helium headwinds), according to CFO Melissa Schaeffer's remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call.

Looking ahead, APD initiated FY 2026 guidance with a midpoint of $13 in adjusted EPS ($12.85 to $13.15). This would represent an 8.1% adjusted EPS growth rate over FY 2025 and was $0.12 above the analyst consensus heading into the Q4 2025 earnings call.

APD anticipates that new asset contributions (e.g., core business assets) will provide gradual and positive volume and earnings support. Additionally, the company anticipates that its base business will deliver strong non-helium pricing growth in FY 2026.

APD also remains committed to becoming a leaner enterprise. When Kody last covered it in October, the company's initial target for its global cost reduction plan was $185 million to $195 million. Now, the company has expanded this to $250 million ($0.90 a share in earnings once the reductions are complete), envisioning a 3,600 headcount reduction, or about 16% of its total workforce. This would be equivalent to its 2018 headcount of around 18,500 people, plus the people that it needs to operate assets that it has added over that time.

Longer term, APD's capex related to its equity contribution to NEOM will be completed in 2027. By that time, it will deliver its first ammonia product and contribute to the company's adjusted EPS in FY 2028 and beyond. This will also drop capex from around $4 billion in FY 2026 to $3 billion in FY 2027. By FY 2028, APD will reach its targeted amount of $2.5 billion.

The company is also in advanced negotiations with the world-leading crop nutrition and ammonia company, Yara International ASA, for marketing and distribution. This is aimed at de-risking the offtake and pricing exposure surrounding the project. Those negotiations are expected to be finalized in the first half of 2026.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to grow 8.1% to $13 in FY 2026. For FY 2027, another 7.6% increase in adjusted EPS to $13.99 is projected. In FY 2028, an additional 8.4% jump in adjusted EPS to $15.17 is anticipated.

APD is financially stable as well. Its net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.2x is enough to garner an A S&P credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook. As major projects eventually come online, this leverage ratio should improve once again (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to APD's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and APD's Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Solid Upside To Fair Value

Since October, shares of APD have declined by 3%. That's a contrast to the 3% total return posted by the S&P 500 index over that time. This has arguably further solidified the value proposition for APD.

Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.7. For more perspective, that's well below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 23.6, according to FAST Graphs. This is also lower than the prior forward 12-month P/E ratio value of 20.1 in October.

Based on conservatism, I'm adjusting Kody's fair value P/E ratio nearly two turns lower from 23.6 to 22. The FAST Graphs annual adjusted EPS growth consensus of 8% would be a bit better than the 10-year CAGR of 6.2% (or comparable to the 7%+ CAGR when adjusting for the impact of the sale of the LNG business).

This updated fair value P/E ratio provides a greater margin of safety for APD's ongoing turnaround. It also accounts for the higher interest rate environment moving forward than has been the case in recent years.

As of today, FY 2026 is about 25% in the books. That means another 75% of FY 2026 and 25% of FY 2027 are yet to come over the next 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month adjusted EPS input of $13.25.

Using my fair value P/E ratio of 22, I arrive at a fair value of $291 a share. Relative to the current $248 share price (as of Dec. 30, 2025), this equates to a 15% discount to fair value.

If APD reverts to my more conservative fair value P/E ratio and matches growth forecasts, it could deliver a 21% total return through calendar year 2026. By the end of FY 2031, APD could log 12% annual total returns. If APD executes and growth accelerates more toward the high end of high single digits, I could see the case for incremental multiple expansion closer to its 10-year average P/E ratio.

Another Modest Dividend Bump Is Around The Corner

APD's 2.9% forward dividend yield registers roughly 110 basis points greater than the materials sector's 1.8% median forward yield. That represents a 30 basis point expansion in the yield spread from October. As a result, Seeking Alpha's Quant System awards B+ and B- grades to APD for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (the former grade was up from a B in October).

APD's dividend is reasonably secure, too. The company's adjusted EPS payout ratio is set to be in the mid-50% range for FY 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, this would be below the 60% payout ratio that rating agencies desire from the industry.

This is why the Zen Research Terminal estimates APD's risk of a dividend cut in the next 12 months to be fairly low at 1.8%. In an average recession, this risk rises to 6.5%. As the company executes on its vision of a smaller employee headcount and capex moderates, the payout should be covered by free cash flow. Such an improvement could lower the risk of a payout cut in the future. In the meantime, APD earns an A grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (unchanged from October and maybe still a bit high).

This puts the Dividend Aristocrat in a decent position to build on its 43-year dividend growth streak. That's vastly superior to the sector median of just 1.1 years. This justifies the Quant System's A+ grade for overall dividend consistency (the same as it was in October).

The Quant System anticipates 2% annual forward dividend per share growth. For the next raise, I believe this is in the ballpark. My estimate is for the quarterly dividend per share to be increased by 2.2% next month to $1.83. That would be below the sector median forecast of 2.9%. This explains the C+ grade from the Quant System for the forward dividend per share growth metric (unchanged from October).

Risks To Consider

APD is a company that I believe will get back on track in FY 2026 and beyond. Yet, there are still risks that could alter or break the investment thesis.

One of the key near-term risks to APD is the potential for persistent helium market headwinds. In FY 2025, the company anticipated that this would weigh on adjusted EPS by $0.50 to $0.55. APD was about in line with expectations, with the actual headwind for the year coming in at $0.49.

CFO Melissa Schaeffer expects essentially the same helium headwind run rate in FY 2026. That's because of the influx of Russian helium moving into Asian markets and the destabilizing effect that the sale of the Bureau of Land Management had on the way the global helium market operates. If these headwinds are further exacerbated by a global softening of demand, that could weigh on APD's financial results.

Another risk to the company remains its international presence. In FY 2025, it conducted business in roughly 50 countries and regions outside the United States. As was the case in FY 2024 as well, that accounted for approximately 60% of APD's total sales (page 13 of 240 of APD's 10-K Filing). This opens it up to a few risks, such as varying regulations, as well as asset seizures and terrorism in less economically/politically stable regions.

One final risk to APD is the potential for key projects like NEOM to encounter cost overruns or delays. If that happens, the company's returns on invested capital would suffer.

Conclusion

APD looks set to return to high single-digit percentage annual adjusted EPS growth in FY 2026 and beyond. Incremental growth projects being placed into service, combined with an expanded global cost reduction plan and productivity/pricing boosts, support this argument. APD's leverage ratio remains healthy, too.

Priced 15% below my fair value estimate, shares appear to be a solid value as well. Collectively, this is why I think that APD can put up 12% annual total returns through FY 2031 and why I'm sticking with Kody's "Buy" rating.

Own Dividend Aristocrats and become dividend royalty - live like a Dividend King.

data.title: MPLX: A Buy Despite Macro Headwinds And Recent Price Strength
data.description:
Introduction

If you have followed along, you might have read my article on the sector winners and losers that I expect for this year. Among the losers, I said that I expect Energy to underperform. Then, this year started, and we are seeing energy stocks soar. XLE is up 4.38% in just a week. We know what triggered this movement: the Venezuelan crisis.

However, while we wait for further developments, I want to explain why I was bearish on energy. I simply considered that inventories were rising at a faster pace than the consumption rate. This has made oil prices move down through 2025, and many believe that we could see them at $55 per barrel this year (currently, we are around $60). With this in mind, the fact that more oil from Venezuela will be available could actually lead to even lower prices.

In this situation, the oil coming from the Permian Basin finds itself at a disadvantage, since its breakeven point is usually higher (according to the EIA, it is between $62 and $64).

I, personally, believe that the U.S. administration is well aware that low oil prices could hurt some of its flagship companies. But, at the same time, I think this is part of a geopolitical strategy to put pressure on Russia. If oil prices stay low, Russia has to sell its oil at even lower prices, thus seeing its revenues decrease. Now, this is an opinion, and I might discuss it in a more thorough way in the following weeks. I thought it was, however, necessary here because it explains why, this year, I am not bullish on drilling (especially if it is U.S.-based). However, I am bullish on midstream companies.

It took me a while to get here so that I could explain how I am approaching MPLX, since this is the company we are going to discuss now.

MPLX LP: Business Model

When we deal with midstream companies, it is always useful to start from a map so that we understand where the company operates, what routes it owns, and its exposure to the main basins in the U.S. MPLX manages both crude oil and natural gas assets. As such, its revenues are mainly fee-based. This means customers sign long-term contracts, which, most of the time, are take-or-pay. Among its customers, MPLX has one that stands out: Marathon Petroleum (MPC), which accounts roughly for 60% of the company's revenues (either directly or indirectly).

This way of making money has an advantage and a disadvantage. The positive is that it usually limits downside, enabling MPLX to have a steady inflow of money that is extremely necessary to cover its high fixed costs. The negative is that growth is also limited. However, what really matters for many unitholders is that a company such as MPLX has a high cash conversion rate.

As a matter of fact, I have sometimes heard that people describe master limited partnerships (more on that in a minute) as a cash annuity, which also offers protection from inflation.

Therefore, it is a mistake to assess MPLX mainly through the lens of oil and natural gas prices or by the fluctuating levels of oil demand. It is much more important to understand production volumes (right now, they are high) and how much refineries are working. MPLX is also a story about interest rates, since they have an impact on both its cost of capital and how competitive government bonds can be compared to its yield (right now, around 8%).

We also see that MPLX has a strong balance sheet: the company has been able to deleverage rapidly and now shows a debt/adj. EBITDA ratio that is just above 3x. Its debt maturities are also well spread out through 2030, with no particular year showing extreme concentration.

Units, Not Shares

Some investors, especially non-U.S. residents, might be a little confused about master limited partnerships. First of all, when you invest in such a company, you are not buying a share but an ownership interest in the partnership itself. This means that the investor becomes a legal partner instead of being a shareholder. The main difference is seen when it comes to cash distribution. While investors are used to collecting dividends after they have been taxed at the corporate level, in the case of MLPs, unitholders receive a distribution, which is basically a return of capital. In this way, these distributions are not taxable right away. They actually reduce the cost basis of the investor while taxes are deferred until the investor sells. This explains why MLPs have really high yields compared to stocks.

There is one caveat, however. Tax filing becomes more complex as MLPs issue a K-1 instead of a 1099. This means that the investor has to do a bit more work when it comes to paying taxes. But many don't mind doing so when they see that, in exchange, they receive extremely high cash flows and have the advantage of deferring taxes. Foreign investors find themselves in another situation. MLPs are somewhat tax-hostile for non-U.S. unitholders. Brokers have to withhold 37% of the distribution because these cash flows are treated as business income (remember, you are a partner). This is why foreigners are better off picking an ETF that handles all of these issues and still allows them to receive nice dividends. The best one I know of is AMLP, on which, needless to say, I am bullish.

MPLX's Valuation

MPLX has been able to change the sentiment around it after the last earnings call. In particular, what bolstered investors was that the company increased its distribution for the second consecutive year by a whopping 12.5%. This also came along with the company saying that it is confident to keep up this pace for at least a few more years.

In fact, Maryann Mannen, its President and CEO, said that:

When we look at our growth rate '24 to '25 and then we look at it also from '25 to '26, we believe '25 to '26 will actually deliver stronger growth than we did '24 to '25. As you know, we've also been talking about our EBITDA growth over a 3-year period. And when we look at that, it's been roughly 7% for the last few years. We see the ability to continue that as we look into 2026 for right now for those acquisitions and other projects that we put into place.

This made MPLX rapidly soar from $48 to $55. Right now, the stock has retraced back a bit, but it is still close to its decade-highs.

This brings us to the most important question: is MPLX still a buy today?

The macro assumption is that volumes might decrease, but not collapse. I believe this scenario is likely to materialize, as I have often explained when dealing with this industry.

When I run a DCF, I don't need very bold assumptions to find out that MPLX still has some decent upside. I plugged in the TTM financials and forecast an FCF of $4.5B at the end of FY25. Then, I assume that its FCF will just grow 2% forever. Right now, its 24M beta is close to 0.50. But over a longer period, it is between 0.70 and 0.80. Sometimes, it has also peaked above 1. So, I used a 0.75 beta. In this way, I calculated a WACC just above 7%. The result is that its units could be worth around $64.50, which means there is a 25% upside, not counting the hefty distributions that MPLX delivers. This is why I think this is one of the most interesting plays in the energy industry, and thus, I rate it a buy.

data.title: LGI: A NAV-Driven Equity CEF, Not An Income Fund
data.description:
Introduction

In a recent article, I discussed Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (PSF). CEFs may come with different mechanisms even if they deliver similar returns. PSF is not a traditional income vehicle but an active timing play on capital structure. Lazard Global Total Return and Income Fund (LGI) is a levered global equity-driven total return fund with emerging market exposure and with distributions driven by NAV performance. Both produce income as a secondary, but from different mechanisms.

LGI is a different type of CEF. PSF is exposed mainly to preferred securities and interest-rate dynamics, whereas LGI buys global equities and adds some spunk to their portfolio with short-duration emerging market currency and debt. The whole thing is enhanced through leverage similar to PSF. That produces a double digit distribution sustained more by NAV behavior than by net investment income coverage.

So while certain aspects are similar, understanding LGI requires a different lens than the one for PSF. The key aspect becomes how global equity and currency exposure produce a stable long-term total return.

The LGI Portfolio

LGI’s portfolio is built around 60-80 US and non-US equity securities, generally with a market cap above $2 billion at the time of purchase. These are large cap businesses selected for strong balance sheets, sustainable returns, strong peer positioning, and sensitivity to the market.

Global equities as of the most recent quarter account for ~69% of total leveraged assets. The tilt is towards high-quality global franchises, not yield producers. Top holdings include Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Amazon (AMZN), ASML (ASML), and Visa (V).

LGI’s portfolio tilts towards growth sectors like information technology, financials, industrials, and health care. There is very little exposure to defensive sectors like utilities and real estate. While 55% of the portfolio is in the North American market, the rest is in Europe and Asia, demonstrating the fund’s global orientation.

The other interesting thing is LGI’s emerging market exposure. Instead of buying long duration bonds, the fund takes a more proactive approach. It seeks income through short-duration emerging-market currency forward contracts and select EM sovereign and corporate debt instruments, typically with durations under one year. This is 30% of the total leveraged asset base, and the idea is to increase income while limiting interest-rate sensitivity. The proportion is large enough to support additional income, but the overall fund performance is still dictated by the global equity NAV dynamic.

Taken together, it is clear that LGI is not an income fund that also owns stocks, but a fund that owns global equity to create a total return strategy, a part of which is income, but is not the only objective.

Leverage, Capital Structure, and Distribution Mechanics

LGI uses leverage to enhance returns. Latest data shows that the fund has a total of $335 million in leveraged assets, against net assets of ~$239 million. So effective leverage is 28-29%, putting LGI squarely in the median of CEFs like PSF, which has a 33% leverage ratio.

LGI uses leverage across its entire portfolio, including both the global equity and the EM currency/debt sleeves. That amplifies the returns across the entire portfolio. Now, fixed income CEFs focus on yield spread - the difference between the return from assets and the cost of funding. This is called the carry, or the yield spread. In fixed income CEFs, leverage only helps scale this spread. In a fund like LGI, there is no focus on yield, so leverage is used for a different purpose here - it accelerates NAV growth by magnifying equity-driven returns in strong markets. Since leverage magnifies both gains and losses, it can also increase losses during stress. In income funds, the situation is different. Here, contractual cash flow offers a cushion during even tough times. That regular cash flow also stops leverage from magnifying losses too much.

The distribution reflects this structural difference. LGI’s current monthly distribution stands at an annualized rate of around $1.84 per share, or ~10% distribution rate on market price. This payout is not controlled by net investment income coverage, but funded through a combination of NII, realized capital gains, and return of capital. This mix varies meaningfully year to year depending on market conditions, as we can see from Section 19(a) disclosures.

In the disclosures from November, we see LGI’s monthly distribution of $0.14770 per share came mostly from realized capital gains, not net investment income. Of that distribution, only 30% ($0.04407) was net investment income, while 70% came from realized capital gains - 5% ($0.00751) from short-term gains and 65% ($0.09612) from long-term gains - with no return of capital. The same behavior is seen in annual data as well - the focus is primarily NAV gains.

Note that LGI’s returns are GAAP recognized only when they are realized. That could create a delay, so the fund may need to pay using ROC for the time being, and coverage may look weak even when the fund has strong earnings. For this reason, payout ratios and other conventional metrics make little sense and offer no insight into distribution sustainability.

We see this from LGI’s distribution history. There is a lot of fluctuation in annual payouts depending on market conditions. For example, annual distributions have ranged from under $1.00 per share in weaker markets to well over $2.00 per share during strong ones. There have been years with large improvements leading to outsized adjustments. More recently, the annualized distribution increased from roughly $1.12 in 2023 to $1.52 in 2024, and further to approximately $1.76-$1.84 in 2025. These show better NAV growth in stronger markets rather than a dependence on NII.

NAV and Market Price Performance

Recent results demonstrate the pros and cons of the NAV based approach rather than income carry.

During November, LGI’s NAV declined 0.1%, while the MSCI All Country World Index (MSCI ACWI) remained flat - a modest underperformance. YTD, LGI’s NAV return of 14.2% trails the MSCI ACWI’s 21.1% gain. Over the last three years, LGI has generated an annualized NAV return of 11.9%, compared with 18.6% for its benchmark index, which blends the MSCI ACWI and MSCI World Index.

This underperformance occurs not because the fund’s structure failed, but because of some divergence between sectors and between regions. In November, the tech sector lost some momentum due to AI-related uncertainties, as well as a lack of clarity in interest rates, compounded by disagreement within the Fed about a December rate cut. The US government shutdown did not help matters.

During this time, developed market equities rose a little, but EM fell, primarily due to weakness in Taiwan and Korea after a strong run. LGI’s NAV simply reflects these developments. The portfolio’s IT stocks helped, but that was offset by weakness in its industrial and health care stocks.

Short duration EM currency and debt performed strongly in November and YTD. This provided some balance, as it was meant to be. So while EM does not drive performance, it can complement and even offer support in poor performance periods. Thus, LGI is an active, equity-focused fund, and not just a benchmark-tracking vehicle. A proper valuation of LGI needs to take this property into account.

Valuation: Discount Matters

LGI is best valued from a market price versus NAV perspective rather than through a reference to yield or coverage. According to the latest Seeking Alpha data, LGI is trading at a 5.64% discount to NAV. This discount remains despite solid performance. In the last one year, LGI delivered a NAV total return of 10.5%, while the market price return was higher at 15.6%. This indicates a partial discount narrowing during strong markets. Over three years, NAV has compounded 11.9% annually, compared with 13.8% on price, which again shows that shareholder returns have been driven by NAV growth and discount compounding and not income alone.

However, investors remain skeptical about LGI’s NAV-driven distributions. This is the reason the fund continues to trade at a discount even though it has a 10.2% distribution rate. They sometimes use ROC, which doesn’t assuage the market. All this makes LGI appear unattractive, even with a strong NAV performance.

This is also seen in the 52-week data. It shows both sentiment and discount influencing how the market looks at LGI. The NAV range in the past year has been between $14.91 and $18.74, while the market price has fluctuated between $13.46 and $17.80. This shows that pricing volatility is not coming from NAV alone, but also from the changing discount levels.

From the above, what we get is that LGI’s valuation is based on three things: a) NAV performance driven by global equity and EM, b) NAV volatility amplified by leverage, and c) market skepticism of NAV-generated distributions. Currently, at a 5.64% discount, LGI is neither deeply distressed nor fully valued. It only reflects an income investor market that keeps discounting equity-driven income strategies despite competitive long-term NAV returns.

Conclusion

LGI and PSF appear similar at some levels - both are leveraged closed-end funds with strong distributions - but they provide those distributions using fundamentally different mechanisms. PSF uses capital-structure timing, where returns and income come from preferred security repricing. LGI is a levered global-equity total-return fund, and here distributions come from NAV growth and capital gains rather than income carry.

At its current discount rate, LGI is neither distressed nor perfectly priced. The discount only comes from market skepticism towards NAV-driven distribution, and not actual NAV performance. For investors who understand the structure and feel comfortable with equity risk and leverage, the skepticism-driven discount is an opportunity. However, I do not prefer NAV-driven, levered equity-focused distribution vehicles in my income portfolio myself. LGI is not a substitute for a traditional income fund. Even the 5% discount does not convince me otherwise. As such, for me, LGI is a hold - meaning, do not buy or add to my income portfolio.

data.title: Bigger Dividends, Better Execution: Choosing Quality For The 2026 MBS Recovery
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

2025 was a fantastic year for agency mortgage REITs. The entire sector showed strength, with the largest REITs in the sector leading the way:

The sector has a lot of tailwinds coming into 2026 as the Federal Reserve's target rate has come down, making the cost of funds cheaper. Meanwhile, mortgage rates have remained relatively high. Agency MBS is the security that originators use to set mortgage rates. Higher mortgage rates mean higher coupons for investors who own agency MBS.

In essence, agency mREITs are receiving more interest on their assets and paying less interest on their debt than they were a couple of years ago. That trend is still going in a positive direction, and naturally, that is a positive for their ability to pay dividends and their share prices.

Additionally, agency MBS consists of mortgages that are guaranteed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. If the borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgages at par value. This means that the asset class has minimal credit risk. The type of risk that agency MBS and, by extension, mREITs that focus on agency MBS, is interest rate risk. MBS prices tend to follow US Treasury prices and are low when long-term Treasury rates are high, like today.

We've talked a lot about Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) and AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC). What if you want to diversify further to take advantage of the natural tailwinds in this sector?

Today, we're going to take a look at two agency mREITs that were in the middle of the pack last year.

DX – Yield 14.5%

Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) is a mortgage REIT that has turned its focus to agency MBS in recent years.

What makes DX different is that it is relatively small. While other mREITs have portfolios that are nearly $100 billion, DX's portfolio was only $15.8 billion in September 2025. Source.

This is both a benefit and a risk. The benefit is that DX is capable of shifting its portfolio very quickly. A smaller portfolio will be much more flexible and capable of quickly changing compared to a much larger portfolio.

The risk to investors is that DX could have a materially different portfolio next quarter than it had last quarter. We have been impressed by watching DX management make decisions through the COVID crisis and through the downswing in agency MBS. Management made wise decisions, and shareholders benefited from those good choices.

The most recent update we have of DX's book value is from the earnings call in October 2025, where management disclosed that book value was $12.71/share. DX was trading at about a 5% premium to book value at that time. We believe book value has come up a bit, but DX is likely still trading at a single-digit premium to book value.

DX's hedging strategy was a bit different from many peers in the early 2020s. DX opted to hedge solely through shorting US Treasuries. As a result, it hedged its book value, but not its cash flow. DX was running in the red with negative cash flow, which was offset by realizing gains on its short positions.

Recently, it has been moving into interest rate swaps, which more directly hedge cash flow in a manner that is more traditional for the sector.

IVR – Yield 17%

Invesco Mortgage Capital Inc. (IVR) is another small mREIT that had a diversified portfolio going into the COVID crisis. Like DX, it can be very flexible with its portfolio and change it on a dime.

IVR's book value was $8.41 at the end of Q3, so very likely it is still trading at a small discount to book value today. It is trading at a cheaper valuation and has a higher yield, so should this be the better option for investors?

Like DX, IVR has a high allocation of agency residential MBS, but has a moderately higher allocation to Agency commercial MBS. Source.

With earnings well in excess of the dividend and trading near or at a slight discount to book value, we can understand why people looking at the numbers might be tempted to choose IVR over DX.

However, this is a sector where management matters, especially when portfolios are relatively small, like they are for these two REITs.

Head To Head: A Look At A Crisis

March 2020 was unique for agency MBS because there were a lot of unknowns and financial dynamics that didn't always follow calm logic. In short, the financial world was panicking. Even agency MBS saw an excessive amount of volatility. Both DX and IVR made trades in their portfolio, trying to navigate the volatility.

The difference in results is stark. It's hard to believe the companies are in the same business:

With the large mREITs that have nearly $100 billion in assets, there will not be a lot of change quarter to quarter. Those are huge ships that require a significant amount of time to reposition their portfolio.

Here is what DX did in early 2020: Source.

Note that in Q1 2020, DX cut its agency MBS (blue) holdings in half and primarily took advantage of relatively low prices in Agency CMBS. Then, within a month, it had significantly rotated back into agency MBS.

If we look at IVR, it went into 2020 with a similar portfolio asset allocation. Roughly half on agency MBS, 22% agency CMBS, and 26% non-agency assets: Source

IVR opted to heavily invest in credit risk, selling off substantially all of its agency MBS assets and increasing its exposure to non-agency commercial credit by 92%. That portion of its portfolio was only 18% before the crisis. Source

Two similar portfolios going into the crisis, but two different management teams making different choices that led to dramatically different results.

DX management used its flexibility to its advantage and outperformed more static peers through the crisis. The larger mREITs will be more stable and are following a more predictable business strategy. We aren't going to see AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) shifting half of its portfolio into a different asset class. We'll see them shift a few billion here or there. DX can shift its portfolio quickly, and in 2020, that proved to be an advantage. IVR is a case study in how management making the right decisions in a crisis is essential.

Conclusion

With our agency MBS investments, we think it is prudent to focus on the behemoths of the sector first. They simply aren't going to change their portfolios dramatically, and are well-equipped to plow through a crisis and come out the other side in similar condition as they went in.

Mortgage REITs with smaller portfolios have the option of being more aggressive and rotating portfolios as conditions change. This can lead to outperformance vs. more static portfolios, but can also lead to catastrophe if management makes poor decisions.

That's why DX is a bonus pick for us as opposed to a core holding. We really like the management; we believe their management has a great track record of making good decisions, and we are very bullish on the agency MBS sector. DX can generate the best return in the sector. However, more execution risk comes with that upside potential. We want to have our core positions that will be boring and predictable, then we'll add on the positions that have the potential for an outsized return but higher risk.

Yet, even looking at the higher-risk option, we want to ensure we are investing in a management team we trust in a tough environment. IVR dropped the ball in 2020, while DX zigged and zagged at the right times. So, even though IVR is trading at a lower valuation and has a higher yield, we prefer to stick with DX.

data.title: Revolution Medicines: Take The Win And Sell Following Large Move Sparked By Buyout Rumors
data.description:
This is my second Revolution Medicines (RVMD) article after 04/2025's "Revolution Medicines: Overpriced RAS-Addicted Cancers Maven". In Maven I rated Revolution as a "Sell". It has since increased by >250%. Enough said, obviously a horrendous call on my part..

Duly chastened, I will now give it another try.

Significance of AbbVie and Merck Acquisition Rumors/Reports

On 01/07/2026, Revolution jumped to $105, up ~30% at its high point on a WSJ report that AbbVie (ABBV) was in late-stage acquisition talks with it. The details were sketchy, but the market for Revolution went up ~30% at its high point; most of the stocks in my speculative biotech portfolio were infected positively by the euphoria. Definitely a risk-on moment for clinical-stage biotechs.

Facially, the report made sense for AbbVie. Oncology drugs as a whole are only contributing 11% to its revenues. Nonetheless, it is not unusual in such situations that takeover rumors do not pan out. That seems to be the case here. Now there are reports that AbbVie is denying any such talks are taking place. Revolution's price is shrinking back. Taking AbbVie at its word, this whole thing may turn out to be just so much smoke. What then for Revolution?

Of course, AbbVie is far from the only big pharma that has needed to beef up their oncology portfolio. Gilead (GILD), the big pharma that I have followed most closely over the years, has worked assiduously but with little to show for the billions it has expended on oncology expansion efforts.

The graphic below from Gilead's Q3/2025 earnings presentation shows how fruitless its efforts have proven:

Its entire oncology portfolio, cell therapy, Trodelvy, and the entire grab bag "other" generated less than $1 billion. It took years and billions to assemble these. The bottom line is Gilead, like many other big pharma companies, would pay dearly for a splashy "win" in the cancer acquisition space. The question I will now address is whether or not Revolution provides solid potential for such a "win".

Subsequent to my writing the foregoing, the Financial Times reported that Merck (MRK) was an expected new suitor with a $28-32 billion price tag. Subsequently, the WSJ issued its own report to the same effect. All this reportage has jacked Revolution's stock price and market cap to historic levels, as shown by its price chart below:

Let's take a look at the prize attracting all this attention. As I discuss below, Revolution's pipeline features four late-stage cancer trials, which are generating this buyout interest.

Revolution's Late-Stage Pipeline is Advancing on Two Key Cancers

Overview

Revolution lists the following as its pipeline on its website accessed on 01/08/2026:

Of its four most advanced programs with treatment by Daraxonrasib (RMC-6236), three are in treatment of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [PDAC]; the fourth is in treatment of non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC].

Its PDAC trials are of the most interest because of the inexorable nature of this often fatal cancer. Surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy are the current principal treatment options.

PDAC Trials—RASolute 302

RASolute 302's phase 3 trial (NCT06625320) is described on clinicaltrials.gov as an:

...open-label, Phase 3 study designed to evaluate whether treatment with RMC-6236 will improve progression free survival [PFS] or overall survival [OS] compared to Investigator's choice of standard of care chemotherapy in patients with metastatic PDAC who were previously treated with one prior line of therapy with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) based or gemcitabine-based regimen.

CEO Goldsmith discusses this trial at length during Revolution's presentation at the 11/18/2025 Jefferies London Healthcare Conference, which is the latest information I've been able to find. He was asked to describe what he would consider success in Revolution's pivotal RASolute 302 trial in second-line PDAC.

He began his response by describing the current situation for patients with PDAC. He noted that virtually all PDAC patients are treated with cytotoxic chemotherapy, resulting in a median PFS on the order of 3 months and a median OS of about 6 months.

Referencing its previous work with roughly 1,000 PDAC patients using different treatment regimens and strategies, he noted:

...based on a monotherapy cohort in second-line pancreatic cancer, we reported a median progression-free survival of over 8 months as compared to the standard of care 3 months and a median overall survival of 13 to 15 months. So really a substantial difference.

Not surprisingly, with such potential benefits, the trial has been enrolling quickly. As of the date of the presentation, he reported that the trial was completely enrolled, with a readout expected in 2026. In response to a question as to interim readouts, Goldsmith noted that it was an event-driven trial based on PFS and OS.

In other words, readouts will not be based on any date certain but rather on the number of overall survival events.

PDAC Trials—RASolute 303 and RASolute 304

During the Jefferies conference, Goldsmith discussed RASolute 303 as Revolution's first phase 3 first-line study in pancreatic cancer. He characterized the trial as having three arms; they are comparing standard-of-care cytotoxic chemotherapy versus daraxonrasib monotherapy versus a combination of daraxonrasib plus chemotherapy.

He went on to note:

It's a very attractive trial design for patients. It means everybody has a 2 out of 3 chance of receiving daraxonrasib. They have a 2 out of 3 chance of receiving chemotherapy as well. We had indicated we expected to initiate that trial in the fourth quarter of this year. I'm also pleased to announce today that we have, in fact, initiated that trial as well. So we're now running 3 global Phase III trials in pancreatic cancer. We're looking to cover every possible indication in pancreatic cancer, and we'll continue to build on that with additional trials.

As for RASolute 304 (NCT07252232), it is a study of Daraxonrasib (RMC-6236) in patients with resected PDAC. CEO Goldsmith described this as:

...for patients with adjuvant disease or -- for adjuvant treatment of resectable disease. That trial we announced a few weeks ago has initiated. Those patients received surgery and perioperative chemotherapy according to standards of care and get randomized either to observation or daraxonrasib monotherapy for a period of 2 years and will follow a disease-free survival for those patients. So that study has been initiated.

This study has an estimated primary completion date of 05/10/2029 and a study completion date of 07/10/2030.

NSCLC Trial—RASolve 301

Revolution's NSCLC phase three trial RASolve 301 (NCT06881784) with an enrollment of 320 patients is titled:

RASolve 301: Phase 3 Multicenter, Open Label, Randomized Study of RMC-6236 Versus Docetaxel in Patients With Previously Treated Locally Advanced or Metastatic RAS[MUT] NSCLC

Its primary completion date is 12/01/2027, with a study completion date of 12/01/2030. This trial was discussed only briefly at the close of the Jefferies conference, with Goldsmith noting that its enrollment was proceeding as expected.

Absent Any Buyout, Revolution Has Significant Liquidity to Operate Its Business

As of 9/30/2025, according to its Q3/2025 10-Q, Revolution had $1.9 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities. As stated by CFO Anders during its Q3/2025 earnings Call, Revolution:

... ended the third quarter of 2025 with $1.93 billion in cash and investments. This balance includes the receipt of the first royalty monetization tranche of $250 million in June 2025 from our partnership with Royalty Pharma, and there remains an additional $1.75 billion in future committed capital under this arrangement.

As reflected by net cash used in operating activities shown in its 10-Q, Revolution had a cash burn of ~$624 million for the first 9 months of 2025, or ~$70 million per month. At this rate, its liquidity should last it an additional ~2 years without further draw on its Royalty Pharma facility.

Conclusion

The sun is shining ever so brightly for Revolution as I write on 01/10/2026. Nonetheless, I rate it as a Sell. In doing so I am cognizant of the following, which temper its overall positive prospects:

  1. while there are reports of deals being considered, no deal has yet to be actually submitted;
  2. absent a deal, Revolution has yet to report topline results from any of its phase 3 trials;
  3. Revolution currently has no FDA-approved product;
  4. Revolution is currently trading with a market cap equivalent to what it would achieve upon fruition of the current rumored deals.

Unhappily, I own no shares in Revolution; if I did, I would sell a significant portion of them and consider myself well served. I might tempt fate in the hope that a bidding war could push shares higher. However, I think this is the case where one investment pundit's often repeated maxim is fully applicable:

Pigs get fat; hogs get slaughtered
